channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: L.A. Kicks Off the World Cup With a Party
item.description: Fans, many draped in flags and colorful costumes, arrived at SoFi Stadium hours before the United States began its campaign against Paraguay.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango and Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times

item.title: One Dead and Several Injured in Tent Collapse Outside Virginia Church
item.description: Severe weather conditions caused the collapse during an outdoor service and concert Friday night.
item.dc:creator: Madaleine Rubin
item.media:credit: Veasey Conway for The New York Times
item.media:description: The EastLake Community Church in Moneta, Va., in 2021.

item.title: Judge Dismisses Republican Group’s Case Against the University of Florida
item.description: A campus group the university banned after accusations of antisemitic behavior failed to prove it had its First Amendment rights violated, the court ruled.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Saul
item.media:credit: Charlotte Kesl for The New York Times
item.media:description: The University of Florida College Republicans came under scrutiny in March.

item.title: Musk Is the World’s First Trillionaire. Who Was the First Billionaire?
item.description: John D. Rockefeller, the Gilded Age oil baron, became America’s first billionaire in 1916. Like Elon Musk’s ascension, the milestone made headlines.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: John D. Rockefeller Sr., in Lakewood, N.J., in 1933. Rockefeller, the Gilded Age oil baron, became a billionaire in 1916.

item.title: Judge Blocks National Parks From Removing ‘Negative’ Signs and Depictions of Slavery
item.description: The ruling accused the Trump administration of engaging in censorship by taking down materials at parks across the country.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: An outdoor exhibit on slavery at Independence National Historical Park in Philadelphia, seen last year, was taken down in January.

item.title: Trump Administration Says It Will Restart Asylum and Immigration Processing
item.description: The response came after a federal judge rebuked officials for failing to immediately comply with the order he issued last week.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo and Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times
item.media:description: An immigration court in New York in May. More than a million applications for asylum and immigration had ground to a halt under Trump administration policies.

item.title: Gavin Newsom and Hunter Biden Would Like Your Attention, Please
item.description: The two men chatted on Mr. Newsom’s podcast about Democrats, Joe Biden, Graham Platner, phone addiction and other topics.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Max Whittaker for The New York Times, Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Gavin Newsom of California hosted Hunter Biden on his podcast this week.

item.title: Trump’s Haphazard Midterm Leadership Puts Republicans in a Bind
item.description: The president and his party have had a rough start to June, after a spring full of MAGA primary victories and redistricting gains.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times

item.title: Two Dan Sullivans? Alaska Republicans Suggest Kicking One Off the Ballot in Senate Race
item.description: The lieutenant governor and the top elections official, both Republicans, are investigating whether the challenger coordinated with a Democrat to confuse voters.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times, Karen Dillman, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Republicans argue that the Senate campaign by Dan J. Sullivan, right, was planned by Democrats to confuse voters and hurt Senator Dan S. Sullivan, left, a second-term lawmaker.

item.title: Despite Trump Claims, Spencer Pratt Seems to Accept L.A. Mayor Results
item.description: President Trump said the loss was an example of election fraud, but Mr. Pratt did not entertain that idea. Instead, he plans to wind down his campaign but keep attacking the two Democrats who advanced.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I didn’t get in this for political power, I got in this to expose this corrupt machine, and nothing’s changed,” Mr. Pratt said. “ I don’t have a campaign laws hamstringing me now. It’s war.”

item.title: Federal Agents Search Voting Rights Group in Ohio
item.description: It was not immediately clear what the investigators were looking for, but a board member with a progressive group said members had been served with search warrants.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Glenn Thrush
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: People voting in Ohio in 2024.

item.title: 26-Year Mystery of a Skeleton in a Tent Ends With DNA Identification
item.description: Remains of the man, Joseph Louis Serrao Jr., discovered in a remote area in Olympic National Park in 2000, were identified using forensic genealogy.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: Olympic National Park in Washington State.

item.title: Atlanta Judge Eleanor Ross Says There Is ‘No Excuse’ For Her Misconduct
item.description: In a second apology letter, Judge Eleanor Ross said her actions were “patently wrong.” Some House Republicans want her impeached.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: C-SPAN
item.media:description: The apologies from Judge Eleanor Ross in Atlanta followed a New York Times report that published on Thursday.

item.title: Judge Declines to Halt UFC Fight at the White House on Trump’s Birthday
item.description: In a ruling on Friday, Judge Amit P. Mehta wrote that the lawsuit arrived last minute and failed to show how the event irreversibly harmed the individuals who sued.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction of a stage for the planned fight at the White House.

item.title: Blocking Trump’s Fund, Judge Presses for Guarantee It Won’t Move Forward
item.description: The ruling was the strongest effort to date to hold the administration to its word that its plans to create the fund have actually been set aside.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Getty Images
item.media:description: President Trump has insisted that he loves the idea of a fund to compensate people claiming to have been unfairly prosecuted by the government, including the rioters who were prosecuted for storming the Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021.

item.title: Suspect in Fatal Shooting Is Dead After a Standoff in Midland, Texas, Officials Say
item.description: The man was confirmed to be dead after he had barricaded himself in a building in Midland, following an earlier shooting on Friday that killed one person and injured 10 others.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman
item.media:credit: KMID/KPEJ, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Law enforcement officers responded to reports of an active shooter in Midland, Texas, on Friday.

item.title: FISA, a Key U.S. Spying Power, Is Expiring. What Does That Mean for Foreign Surveillance?
item.description: Top officials and some lawmakers say that letting a powerful spying authority expire leaves the United States dangerously blind. But surveillance can still continue.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz
item.category: Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson said that not extending the law, known as Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, risked “a serious calamity on our shores.”

item.title: Mexico’s police focus on World Cup while thousands remain missing.
item.dc:creator: Brent McDonald, Souleyman Messalti, Miguel Tovar, Ben Laffin and Mark Boyer
item.media:credit: Brent McDonald/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ana Hatsumi Muñoz, a member of the Guerreros Buscadores search collective, stands before an altar for four of her family members that have disappeared or been murdered in recent years.

item.title: Fact-Check: Trump’s Claims About His Arch, Reflecting Pool Repairs and Other Renovation Projects
item.description: President Trump has made false or exaggerated claims of a Civil War-era push for a triumphal arch, hundreds of millions spent on repairs on the Reflecting Pool, and an absence of working fountains.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu and Jamie Leventhal

item.title: Rick Jackson, Georgia Governor Candidate, Is Also a Film Producer Battling the IRS
item.description: Rick Jackson, a billionaire, wants to be Georgia’s next governor. But the I.R.S. has questioned how his low-budget movies turned into tax windfalls.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim
item.media:credit: Dustin Chambers for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rick Jackson has sought $90 million in tax deductions from four money-losing films.

item.title: To Defeat Democrats, Texas Governor Embraces the Hard Right
item.description: After years of keeping his distance, Gov. Greg Abbott is set to address Texas Republicans’ activist-led convention as hard-line conservatives gain influence within the party.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
item.media:description: Governor Greg Abbott addressed the Republican Party of Texas convention in Houston on Friday.

item.title: World Cup Ticket Prices in Mexico City Too Expensive for Most Fans
item.description: The opening game in Mexico City took place amid weeks of protests from groups including teachers’ unions complaining about pay and pensions.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans sing the national anthem at Estadio Azteca in Mexico City before the opening match of the FIFA World Cup on Thursday.

item.title: Ohio City Worker Union Complains That Goats Are Eating Its Lunch
item.description: A union in Columbus made a formal complaint after the city farmed out brush-clearing duties at a wastewater plant. The competitors? Goats.
item.dc:creator: Sejal Govindarao
item.category: American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees

item.title: Sheriffs in Maryland Challenge State Limits on Cooperation With ICE
item.description: A lawsuit by a group of 17 county law enforcement officers is another front in the Trump-era fight over local police’s role in immigration enforcement.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Maryland’s Community Trust Act, among other measures, bars local officials from asking people about their immigration status.

item.title: Frisco Mayor’s Race Tests Anti-Muslim Politics in Texas
item.description: A runoff in the Dallas suburb of Frisco is testing whether anti-Muslim rhetoric, prominent in G.O.P. primaries this year, can win over a broader set of voters.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Campaign signs for and against the mayoral candidate Rod Vilhauer in Frisco, Texas.

item.title: The World Cup Kicks Off in America With a Mix of Fretting and Fervor
item.description: Astronomical ticket prices, soaring security costs and concern over traffic and transit snarls is mixed with pride in host cities and excitement over the U.S. team.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The United States plays Paraguay on Friday in the first U.S.-hosted game of the World Cup, at SoFi Stadium near Los Angeles. FIFA is requiring stadiums to hide the logos of their corporate sponsors during the tournament.

item.title: It’s Time to Worry About Ticks in the City
item.description: Think ticks are limited to rural areas and the wilderness? Think again.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Kate Warren for The New York Times
item.media:description: A blacklegged tick, which researchers say is the most dominant species, can be found in most city parks.

item.title: Inside Cesar Chavez’s Labor Union, Women Faced a Culture of Sexual Abuse
item.description: Many of the women who helped lead the fight for Latino civil rights say they were quietly waging their own battles with harassment, misogyny and sexual assault inside the powerful United Farm Workers union.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Hurtes and Manny Fernandez
item.media:credit: Cayce Clifford for The New York Times
